justification: All pass criteria met: (1) Atomic acceptance identified and solved via Redis SETNX with Lua script, (2) In-memory Redis state recommended with explicit durability trade-off acknowledged ('Redis failure loses all active bid windows' risk + trade-off table entry on Redis SETNX gains vs sacrifices), (3) Fallback auto-assignment on timer expiry designed via sorted set timer + highest-rated courier selection, (4) Hot key / scalability risk addressed via zone partitioning discussion and thundering herd analysis, (5) Durability vs latency trade-off explicitly covered in trade-offs table (Redis SETNX row: 'Redis becomes a SPOF' sacrifice, ephemeral 15s windows vs persistence).

observations.text: Excellent atomic acceptance design using Redis SETNX with a Lua script that handles both the accept and timeout paths through the same atomic operation, eliminating race conditions
Comprehensive trade-offs table with alternatives considered for each decision, including explicit rejection of PostgreSQL for atomic claims with latency reasoning
Timer race condition at t=14.9s/t=15.0s explicitly addressed — both accept and timeout use SETNX so Redis serializes them
Hot key / zone scalability addressed through zone-based partitioning strategy with each zone getting its own Redis keyspace

Fallback auto-assignment clearly designed: timer fires via sorted set polling, triggers highest-rated available courier selection, with open questions about edge cases (zero available couriers) correctly flagged as domain decisions
Clock skew risk identified and mitigated by co-locating timer with bid coordinator and using Redis TIME as single clock source
Pitfalls section explicitly warns against database-first approaches with clear reasoning

Thundering herd analysis dismisses the concern at 500 windows but doesn't deeply explore Redis hot key problem if all 500 windows land on the same shard — zone partitioning is mentioned as a flexibility point but not as a direct mitigation for hot keys

agent_output_excerpt:
# Courier Bidding System — Architecture Design

## Executive Summary

The courier bidding system is a **time-bounded, first-writer-wins concurrency problem** at its core. The architecture centers on Redis as the atomic coordination layer for bid windows, WebSockets for real-time courier communication, and a timer service for deadline enforcement. The scale (500 concurrent windows × 30 couriers = ~15K peak WebSocket connections) is well within single-cluster capacity — the real challenge is corre
